conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with the first question: **Do you have any children you're responsible for?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know about Alex.

Now, to continue assessing your eligibility, could you please tell me: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. 

Next question: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just a few more questions to make sure I have all the information I need.

**Is Alex in hospital, in care, or in any residential accommodation, or has he been for an extended period recently?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, he was in care for a short period recently - just a couple of months. But he's back home with me now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. This is important information for determining your eligibility.

I need to understand the timing a bit better. **How long ago did Alex return home from care, and when he was in care, did he spend any regular time at home (such as weekends or overnight visits)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He came back home recently - he was in care for about 7 weeks in total. While he was there, he didn't come home for visits - he was there the whole time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. That's helpful information.

Now, one final question: **Are you or your partner (if you have one) earning an individual income over £60,000 per year?**

(This won't affect whether you can *get* Child Benefit, but it may mean you need to pay a tax charge on it.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us earns over £60,000 a year, so that shouldn't be an issue.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:

- You have an 8-year-old son, Alex, who lives with you in the UK
- You are responsible for him and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- He was in care for about 7 weeks (which is under the 8-week threshold)
- He's now back home with you
- Your income is under £60,000

According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. While Alex was in care for 7 weeks without coming home, this is under the 8-week limit after which Child Benefit would normally stop. Since he's now back home with you full-time, you should be eligible to claim or continue claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

You meet all the requirements:
- Alex is under 16 (he's 8 years old)
- You live in the UK
- You're responsible for Alex and he lives with you
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him

The fact that Alex was in care for 7 weeks doesn't affect your eligibility now that he's back home with you. Child Benefit can continue for up to 8 weeks when a child is in care, and since his stay was under that threshold and he's now returned home, you can claim or continue to receive Child Benefit.

If you haven't already claimed, you can apply for Child Benefit through the government's online service or by completing the relevant forms. If you had been receiving it before Alex went into care, it should continue now that he's home.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK (Manchester), and the claimant is responsible for him. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex was in care for approximately 7 weeks, this is under the 8-week threshold specified in the rules after which Child Benefit entitlement would cease. He has now returned home. The claimant meets all the basic eligibility criteria: they are responsible for a child under 16, they live in the UK, and they live with the child. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. They meet all the standard eligibility requirements: they live in the UK, are responsible for Alex who is under 16, live with him, and no one else is claiming for him. Alex's recent 7-week stay in care does not affect current eligibility as it was under the 8-week threshold and he has returned home.

The claimant meets all the basic eligibility criteria: they are responsible for a child under 16, they live in the UK, and they live with the child. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
